text.title: database-design Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for database-design as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: BEGIN; CREATE INDEX idx_... ON large_table(...); COMMIT;
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside a transaction. Using it in a BEGIN/COMMIT block silently falls back to the locking version.

failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: The 255-byte limit is a MySQL pre-5.0.3 artifact with no meaning in PostgreSQL. Use TEXT with explicit CHECK constraints only when a length limit is a business requirement.

failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: FK overhead is negligible for most workloads. Without a benchmark showing >10% impact, removing FKs silently degrades data integrity.

failure-mode.description: Proposing materialized views without a refresh job and schedule.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: A materialized view without refresh becomes stale immediately after creation. Require a documented refresh strategy (CONCURRENTLY + schedule) in the same PR.

failure-mode.description: Audit trigger using current_user for the app-level user ID.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: current_user is the database role (e.g., app_user), not the application user (e.g., user ID 42). Wire app-level identity via SET LOCAL app.user_id = $1 in the transaction.
